# Experiments

## Evaluation Setup

We evaluate Pytifex across six independent pipeline runs conducted between February and April 2026, testing four type checkers spanning the maturity spectrum: the established
mypy (v1.19.0), the recently released pyrefly (v0.44.2) and zuban (v0.3.0), and the alpha-stage ty (v0.0.1-alpha.32). Each run mined fresh seed bugs from type checker
repositories, generated candidate programs via Gemini 2.5 Flash, filtered for disagreements, and evaluated the results through the comprehensive phased evaluation system
described in Section 3.

From the combined output of all runs, we randomly curated a set of 25 example files whose evaluation verdicts were manually verified for accuracy. The curation process involved
inspecting the source code of each generated program, cross-referencing checker outputs against PEP specifications, and verifying that runtime behavior matched the phased
oracle's assessment. Two candidate files were excluded because their Phase 2 (Hypothesis) evidence was questionable: `loop-newtype-divergence.py` produced `ValueError`
exceptions from Hypothesis-generated edge case inputs (empty tuples) rather than genuine type errors, and `mixed-tuple-unpacking-newtype.py` exhibited similar false triggers
from tuple length mismatches. Both files were excluded to ensure that every reported verdict in the curated set is defensible.

The 25 curated files span four confidence phases. Nine files reached Phase 1 (runtime crash detection), representing the highest confidence verdicts where executing the
generated code deterministically produces a `TypeError`, `KeyError`, or `AttributeError`. Four files reached Phase 2 (Hypothesis property-based testing), where the evaluation
system discovered type violations by generating random inputs matching declared type signatures in the generated source files and catching runtime exceptions. Three files were
resolved by Phase 0 (AST-based oracle), where static analysis independently identified a PEP violation and checked whether each checker flagged it. Seven files reached Phase 3
(PEP specification compliance), where verdicts are derived from matching checker behavior against specific PEP requirements. Two files remained genuinely UNCERTAIN after all
evaluation phases, representing areas where the Python typing specification is ambiguous and checkers make legitimately different design choices.

Table 4.1 presents the code complexity metrics for all 25 curated files. The generated programs range from 5 to 62 lines of code (excluding comments and blank lines), with a
median of 26 LOC. Type annotation density which is the ratio of annotated to unannotated objects, normalized by lines of code, ranges from -0.04 to 0.48, with negative values
indicating files where unannotated assignments outnumber typed parameters.

**Table 4.1: Code complexity metrics for the 25 curated examples.**

| File | LOC | Funcs | Type Imports | Density | Calls |
|:-----|----:|------:|-------------:|--------:|------:|
| decorator-super-modified-sig. | 20 | 4 | 5 | 0.30 | 0 |
| typevartuple-unpacking-prec. | 26 | 2 | 8 | -0.04 | 1 |
| dataclass-transform-param. | 31 | 3 | 6 | -0.03 | 0 |
| inherited-unsound-helper | 35 | 7 | 4 | 0.26 | 0 |
| isinstance-protocol-div. | 57 | 5 | 6 | 0.16 | 1 |
| typeddict-unpack-total-false | 20 | 1 | 3 | 0.30 | 0 |
| typeddict-unpack-notrequired | 39 | 1 | 4 | 0.15 | 0 |
| typeddict-unpack-nested | 33 | 1 | 3 | 0.18 | 0 |
| typevartuple-generic-nested | 23 | 5 | 5 | 0.48 | 0 |
| typeguard-self-protocol | 56 | 9 | 7 | 0.23 | 0 |
| typeis-variance-divergence | 23 | 3 | 4 | 0.13 | 0 |
| typeis-decorated-method | 22 | 4 | 5 | 0.18 | 0 |
| decorator-super-generic-args | 20 | 4 | 5 | 0.25 | 0 |
| walrus-comprehension | 5 | 0 | 0 | 0.00 | 0 |
| typeddict-single-field | 5 | 1 | 1 | 0.00 | 0 |
| literal-inference-divergence | 9 | 1 | 1 | 0.00 | 0 |
| literal-narrowing | 16 | 4 | 2 | 0.13 | 3 |
| list-concat-generic-self | 15 | 2 | 4 | 0.27 | 0 |
| match-typeguard-generic | 33 | 3 | 5 | 0.12 | 2 |
| match-protocol-generic-type | 47 | 13 | 5 | 0.26 | 0 |
| typeddict-inherit-total-div. | 62 | 5 | 4 | 0.16 | 0 |
| asyncio-generic-protocol-cb. | 35 | 8 | 6 | 0.23 | 3 |
| typeguard-generic-list-newtype | 35 | 3 | 6 | 0.14 | 2 |
| unsound-self-cast-divergence | 50 | 6 | 7 | 0.02 | 0 |
| typeddict-total-inheritance | 62 | 2 | 8 | 0.11 | 1 |

## Results Overview

Table 4.2 summarizes the per-checker accuracy across all 25 curated examples. Accuracy is computed as the ratio of CORRECT verdicts to non-UNCERTAIN verdicts (i.e., cases
where the evaluation system reached a definitive conclusion).

**Table 4.2: Per-checker accuracy across the curated evaluation set.**

| Checker | CORRECT | INCORRECT | UNCERTAIN | Total | Accuracy |
|:--------|--------:|----------:|----------:|------:|---------:|
| mypy | 18 | 5 | 2 | 25 | 78.3% |
| zuban | 16 | 7 | 2 | 25 | 69.6% |
| pyrefly | 12 | 11 | 2 | 25 | 52.2% |
| ty | 1 | 21 | 3 | 25 | 4.5% |

Mypy achieves the highest accuracy at 78.3%, correctly handling 18 of 23 decidable cases. Zuban follows at 69.6% (16 of 23), pyrefly at 52.2% (12 of 23), and ty at 4.5% (1
of 22). The disparity between mypy and ty reflects the maturity gap: mypy has been under active development since 2012 with over 10,000 test cases in its suite, while ty was
released as an alpha in early 2026.

### Disagreement Structure

Table 4.3 presents the checker status (ok or error) and disagreement split pattern for each curated file.

**Table 4.3: Checker statuses and disagreement split patterns.**

| File | mypy | pyrefly | zuban | ty | Split |
|:-----|:----:|:-------:|:-----:|:--:|:-----:|
| decorator-super-modified-sig. | error | error | error | ok | 3-1 |
| typevartuple-unpacking-prec. | error | error | error | ok | 3-1 |
| dataclass-transform-param. | error | error | error | ok | 3-1 |
| inherited-unsound-helper | error | error | error | ok | 3-1 |
| isinstance-protocol-div. | error | ok | ok | ok | 1-3 |
| typeddict-unpack-total-false | error | error | error | ok | 3-1 |
| typeddict-unpack-notrequired | error | error | error | ok | 3-1 |
| typeddict-unpack-nested | error | error | error | ok | 3-1 |
| typevartuple-generic-nested | error | error | error | ok | 3-1 |
| typeguard-self-protocol | error | error | error | ok | 3-1 |
| typeis-variance-divergence | error | ok | error | ok | 2-2 |
| typeis-decorated-method | error | error | error | ok | 3-1 |
| decorator-super-generic-args | ok | ok | ok | error | 1-3 |
| walrus-comprehension | ok | error | ok | error | 2-2 |
| typeddict-single-field | error | error | error | error | 0-4 |
| literal-inference-divergence | error | error | error | ok | 3-1 |
| list-concat-generic-self | error | ok | error | ok | 2-2 |
| match-typeguard-generic | error | error | ok | error | 3-1 |
| match-protocol-generic-type | error | error | error | ok | 3-1 |
| literal-narrowing | error | ok | ok | error | 2-2 |
| typeddict-inherit-total-div. | ok | error | error | ok | 2-2 |
| asyncio-generic-protocol-cb. | error | ok | error | ok | 2-2 |
| typeguard-generic-list-newtype | error | error | ok | ok | 2-2 |
| unsound-self-cast-divergence | ok | error | ok | error | 2-2 |
| typeddict-total-inheritance | error | ok | error | error | 3-1 |

The dominant disagreement pattern is the 3:1 split, where three checkers agree on a status (typically `error`) and one dissents (typically `ok`). Table 4.4 shows the
distribution of split patterns across the curated set.

**Table 4.4: Distribution of disagreement split patterns.**

| Pattern | Count | % | Examples |
|:--------|------:|--:|:---------|
| 3 error / 1 ok | 14 | 56.0% | decorator-super-modified-sig. (+13) |
| 2 error / 2 ok | 8 | 32.0% | typeis-variance, walrus, literal-narrowing, typeddict-inherit (+4) |
| 1 error / 3 ok | 2 | 8.0% | isinstance-protocol, decorator-super-generic |
| 4 error / 0 ok | 1 | 4.0% | typeddict-single-field |

The prevalence of 3:1 splits (56.0%) suggests that disagreements are typically caused by a single checker deviating from a widely shared interpretation, rather than by
fundamental ambiguities that split the ecosystem evenly. The eight 2:2 splits represent genuinely contested areas where the ecosystem is divided: TypeIs variance (PEP 742),
walrus operator scoping (PEP 572), Literal narrowing from `in` checks (PEP 586), TypedDict totality inheritance (PEP 589), asyncio Protocol callback signatures, TypeGuard
with NewType in generic containers, generic list concatenation variance (PEP 484), and unsound cast semantics (PEP 673). Notably, in six of these eight 2-2 splits the phased
evaluation correctly determined which side was right, demonstrating that the tiered oracle can resolve contested cases even when consensus-based approaches would fail.

### Minority Analysis

Table 4.5 reveals which checker most frequently occupies the minority position in asymmetric splits (3:1 and 1:3).

**Table 4.5: Minority checker frequency and correctness.**

| Checker | Minority | As Error | As OK | Correct | Incorrect | Uncertain |
|:--------|:--------:|---------:|------:|--------:|----------:|----------:|
| ty | 12 | 1 | 11 | 0 | 11 | 1 |
| mypy | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| pyrefly | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| zuban | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 |

Ty appears as the sole minority checker in 12 of 15 minority cases (80.0%), and was INCORRECT in 11 of those 12 instances. In 11 of 12 cases, ty occupied the minority as `ok`
which means it accepted code that the other three checkers flagged as erroneous. The single case where ty appeared as the minority with `error` (`decorator-super-generic-args`)
was also an INCORRECT verdict: ty flagged an unresolved attribute on a `ParamSpec`-decorated callable while all three other checkers correctly accepted the code. Mypy,
pyrefly, and zuban each appear as the minority checker in exactly one case. This concentration of minority appearances in ty is consistent with its alpha status and limited
conformance coverage, which independent testing places at 15% [@sinon2025conformance].

## Runtime-Proven False Negatives (Phase 1)

Nine of the 21 curated files produce deterministic runtime crashes when executed, providing the highest confidence evidence of type checker bugs. Table 4.6 details the
runtime errors and which checkers caught them.

**Table 4.6: Runtime crash detection results (Tier 1). ✓ = caught the bug, ✗ = missed, Partial = flagged a related but not the crash-site error.**

| File | Error | mypy | pyrefly | zuban | ty |
|:-----|:------|:----:|:-------:|:-----:|:--:|
| decorator-super-modified-sig. | TypeError | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ |
| typevartuple-unpacking-prec. | TypeError | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ |
| dataclass-transform-param. | TypeError | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ |
| inherited-unsound-helper | AttrError | Partial | ✗ | Partial | ✗ |
| isinstance-protocol-div. | TypeError | Partial | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |
| typeddict-unpack-total-false | TypeError | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ |
| typeddict-unpack-notrequired | TypeError | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |
| typeddict-unpack-nested | KeyError | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |
| typevartuple-generic-nested | TypeError | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ |

In seven of these nine files, ty was the sole checker to miss the crash. It reported `ok` while the code raises `TypeError` or `KeyError` at runtime. In the remaining two
files (`inherited-unsound-helper` and `isinstance-protocol-divergence`), all four checkers failed to detect the bug, exposing blind spots shared across the entire ecosystem.

### TypedDict and Unpack (PEP 692)

Three files exercise TypedDict combined with `Unpack`, a construct that allows TypedDict types to specify keyword argument types for functions. In `typeddict-unpack-total-false-extra`,
a TypedDict with `total=False` is used with `Unpack` to define function keyword arguments and pass extra keywords that the TypedDict does not declare triggers a `TypeError`
at runtime. Mypy, pyrefly, and zuban all correctly reject the call, but ty accepts it silently. In `typeddict-unpack-notrequired`, a similar pattern with `NotRequired` fields
reveals a more nuanced split: only mypy correctly rejects the call when a required field is omitted, while pyrefly, zuban, and ty all miss the violation. The third file,
`typeddict-unpack-nested` accesses an `extra_config` key on a TypedDict that does not declare it, producing a `KeyError`. In this case also, only mypy catches the invalid
key access while the other three checkers accept it.

This cluster of TypedDict findings demonstrates that even among the three checkers that generally perform well on Phase 1 cases, the interaction between TypedDict, `Unpack`,
`NotRequired`, and `extra_items` exposes significant implementation gaps.

### TypeVarTuple (PEP 646)

Two files test TypeVarTuple handling. The file `typevartuple-unpacking-precision` uses a generic helper function with an explicit `List[Union[*Ts]]` return type that triggers
a `TypeError` at runtime due to incorrect TypeVarTuple instantiation. The file `typevartuple-generic-class-nested-func` attempts to use both PEP 695 syntax (`class ConfigStore[*Ts]`)
and explicit `Generic[*Ts]` inheritance simultaneously, resulting in a redundant generic base class error at import time. In both cases, mypy, pyrefly, and zuban correctly
report errors while ty reports no issues.

### Decorator Signatures and dataclass_transform

The file `decorator-super-modified-signature` demonstrates a decorator that modifies a method's signature via `Concatenate` (PEP 612), causing a call-site mismatch that
crashes with `TypeError: Derived.process_data() missing 1 required positional argument`. The file `dataclass-transform-param-spec-return` combines `@dataclass_transform`
(PEP 681) with `ParamSpec`-based return types, producing a type error that three checkers catch but ty does not. Both files are seeded from real bug reports
(astral-sh/ty#2646 and pyright#11015), illustrating the pipeline's ability to generate novel variations from historical patterns.

### Shared Blind Spots

Two files expose cases where all four checkers fail. The file `isinstance-protocol-divergence` defines a `Barkable` Protocol and uses it as the second argument to `isinstance()`.
PEP 544 specifies that runtime checkable protocols require the `@runtime_checkable` decorator without it `isinstance()` raises `TypeError`. While mypy warns about the type
argument, it still allows the code to proceed to the crash site without flagging the actual `isinstance` call. Pyrefly, zuban, and ty emit no relevant warnings.

The file `inherited-unsound-helper` exposes a limitation in how type checkers handle the `Self` type (PEP 673) in inherited factory methods. A subclass overrides a factory
method to return `BasicFactory[str]` instead of `Self`, causing an `AttributeError` when a subclass expects `Self`-typed attributes. All four checkers report errors related
to the return type mismatch, but none catches the downstream `AttributeError` that occurs when the unsound return value is used. This represents a systemic gap: checkers
validate the return type annotation in isolation but do not propagate the consequence of an unsound return through subsequent attribute accesses.

## Hypothesis Property-Based Testing (Phase 2)

Four files are evaluated at Phase 2, where Hypothesis generates random inputs matching declared type signatures in the generated source files and calls the generated
functions to discover runtime type violations that Phase 1 (direct execution) did not trigger. Unlike Phase 1, which executes the program's `__main__` block as-is, Phase 2
exercises code paths with adversarial inputs that the LLM-generated test harness may not have covered. Using this method the phased evaluation tries to discover as many code
paths as possible in the mutated source files to explore possible `TypeError`s.

### TypeGuard with Self in Protocol (PEP 647)

The file `typeguard-self-protocol-refined` defines a `Validatable` Protocol with a `TypeGuard[Self]` method and two concrete implementations (`User` and `Product`).
Hypothesis attempts to instantiate `Validatable` directly — which is prohibited by PEP 544 — triggering `TypeError: Protocols cannot be instantiated`. This is a real type
safety violation because any code path that constructs a `Validatable()` will crash at runtime. Mypy, pyrefly, and zuban all flag Protocol related errors in the code while ty
does not. It makes ty the sole INCORRECT checker for this given file.

### TypeIs Variance Divergence (PEP 742)

The file `typeis-variance-divergence-refined` declares a covariant `TypeVar` (`T_co`) and uses it as a type parameter for a class inheriting from `list[T_co]`. Since `list`
is invariant, this creates a type-unsafe situation. Hypothesis discovers the violation by attempting to construct `MyContainer()` with keyword arguments that `list.__init__`
does not accept, triggering `TypeError: list() takes no keyword arguments`. Mypy and zuban correctly flag the variance violation; pyrefly and ty accept the code silently.

### TypeIs with Decorated Method (PEP 742)

The file `typeis-decorated-method-refined` wraps a `TypeIs` function with a `ParamSpec`-based decorator, creating a semantic mismatch: `TypeIs[list[int]]` on a method should
narrow the first positional argument (`self`), but the function's logic operates on the second argument (`x`). Hypothesis discovers that calling the decorated method with
generated inputs triggers `TypeError: Validator.is_list_of_ints() missing 1 required positional argument`. Mypy, pyrefly, and zuban all report errors; ty accepts the code.

### ParamSpec Decorator with super() (PEP 612)

The file `decorator-super-generic-args` applies a `ParamSpec`-based decorator (`trace_method`) to a method that calls `super().greet()`. The decorator preserves the
signature, but Hypothesis discovers that calling `Derived.greet()` without arguments triggers `TypeError: Derived.greet() missing 1 required positional argument: 'name'`. The
decorator's wrapper function consumes `self` but does not correctly forward the remaining arguments to `super().greet()`. All four checkers miss this bug: mypy, pyrefly, and
zuban accept the code without errors, and while ty flags an unresolved `__qualname__` attribute on the wrapped callable, it does not detect the failure that is related to the
argument forwarding problem. This file represents one of the two cases where all four checkers received INCORRECT verdicts.

## PEP Specification Compliance (Phase 3) and Oracle (Phase 0)

Six files are evaluated at Phase 3 (PEP compliance) or Phase 0 (AST oracle), where verdicts are derived from matching checker behavior against specific PEP requirements. These
files do not crash at runtime but contain constructs whose correct handling is prescribed by Python Enhancement Proposals.

### Walrus Operator Scope (PEP 572)

The file `walrus_comprehension.py` provides a minimal five-line
test case that exposes a scoping disagreement:

**Code Listing 4.1: Walrus operator in list comprehension
(PEP 572).**
```{python}
lengths = list(range(5))
cumsum = 0
cs = [cumsum := cumsum + x for x in lengths]
print(cs)
print(cumsum)
```

PEP 572 specifies that the walrus operator (`:=`) in a comprehension targets the enclosing scope, and that the right-hand side of the assignment expression may reference
variables from that scope. The variable `cumsum` is initialized to `0` in the enclosing scope before the comprehension executes. Mypy and zuban correctly accept this code
without errors. However, pyrefly reports `cumsum is uninitialized` and ty reports `Name cumsum used when not defined`, both incorrectly treating the right-hand side reference
to `cumsum` within the comprehension as an unresolved name despite its initialization in the enclosing scope. This small code example, seeded from ty issue #3077, demonstrates
that even simple language features can expose fundamental scoping model differences between type checkers.

### TypedDict Key Inference (PEP 589)

The file `typeddict_single_field.py` tests what type a checker reveals when accessing a key which is not present in the TypedDict definition. PEP 589 specifies that such
access is an error. All four checkers correctly report the invalid key access. However, when revealing the type of the expression `td["anything"]`, mypy and zuban report
`Any`, pyrefly reports `Unknown`, and ty reports `str`. The first three behaviors are acceptable — `Any` or `Unknown` for an erroneous expression — but ty's inference of
`str` implies that it resolves the invalid key to the value type of the TypedDict's declared fields, which could mask downstream type errors if the expression is used in
further computation.

### Literal Inference Ambiguity (PEP 586)

The file `literal-inference-divergence` defines a class with an untyped attribute `DEFAULT_MODE = "production"` and passes it to a function expecting `Literal["production", "development", "staging"]`.
Mypy, pyrefly, and zuban all report a type error because the attribute is inferred as `str`, not `Literal["production"]`. Ty accepts the code silently, inferring the
attribute as a Literal type. PEP 586 allows but does not mandate Literal inference for variables initialized with literal values, making this a genuine design difference
rather than a clear bug. We therefore assign ty an UNCERTAIN verdict for this file — its behavior is arguably more precise but diverges from the conservative approach adopted
by the other three checkers.

### Generic List Concatenation (PEP 484)

The file `list-concat-generic-self` defines a `BaseContainer[T]` and a `SpecializedContainer` subclass, then concatenates lists of each:

**Code Listing 4.2: Generic list concatenation with non-generic subclass.**
```{python}
class BaseContainer(Generic[T]):
    def __init__(self, data: T):
        self.data = data

class SpecializedContainer(BaseContainer[T]):
    pass

base_list: list[BaseContainer[str]] = [BaseContainer("hello")]
specialized_list: list[SpecializedContainer[str]] = [SpecializedContainer("world")]
combined_list: list[BaseContainer[str]] = base_list + specialized_list
```

The AST oracle (Phase 0) identifies that `SpecializedContainer` is subscripted with type arguments despite not being independently generic — it inherits from `BaseContainer[T]`
but does not declare its own type parameters. PEP 484 specifies that only classes that are explicitly generic (via `Generic[T]` or PEP 695 syntax) may be subscripted. Mypy and
zuban correctly flag the unsupported operand types for the `+` concatenation, while pyrefly and ty accept it without error. This is a 2-2 split where the phased evaluation
correctly identifies mypy and zuban as CORRECT and pyrefly and ty as INCORRECT through the oracle's structural analysis.

### Literal Narrowing via Membership Tests (PEP 586)

The file `literal_narrowing.py`, seeded from mypy issue #21090, tests whether type checkers narrow a `str` variable to `Literal["a", "b"]` after an `if foo in ("a", "b")`
guard. PEP 586 does not explicitly mandate this narrowing, but the semantics are clear: after the guard, `foo` can only be `"a"` or `"b"`. The code then passes `foo` to a
function expecting `Literal["a", "b"]`:

**Code Listing 4.3: Literal narrowing via `in` check (PEP 586).**
```{python}
def bar(p: Literal["a", "b"]) -> None: ...

def positive_in(foo: str):
    if foo in ("a", "b"):
        bar(foo)  # Should be accepted after narrowing
```

Pyrefly and zuban correctly narrow `foo` to `Literal["a", "b"]` and accept the call. Mypy and ty do not perform this narrowing, leaving `foo` as `str` and flagging the call
as an `arg-type` error. This is a 2-2 split where the phased evaluation identifies pyrefly and zuban as CORRECT (they correctly narrow) and mypy and ty as INCORRECT (they miss
a valid narrowing opportunity). This result is notable because mypy — the most mature checker — is on the wrong side, demonstrating that maturity does not guarantee correctness
on every feature.

### TypedDict Inheritance and Totality at Runtime (PEP 589)

The file `typeddict-inheritance-total-divergence` creates a three-level TypedDict hierarchy where `BaseSettings(total=True)` declares required fields, `ExtendedSettings(total=False)`
inherits them and adds optional fields, and `FinalSettings(total=True)` re-establishes totality. A dict literal missing the `api_key` field is annotated as `FinalSettings`,
and the code crashes with a `KeyError` when the missing field is accessed at runtime. Pyrefly and zuban correctly flag the missing key, while mypy and ty do not — a 2-2 split
resolved at Phase 1 (runtime crash) with pyrefly and zuban CORRECT and mypy and ty INCORRECT.

### asyncio Protocol Callback Signatures

The file `asyncio-generic-protocol-callback` defines a `TaskResultProcessor` Protocol with a `process_result` method that accepts an `asyncio.Task[T]` and an optional
`metadata` string parameter. When the method is passed directly to `task.add_done_callback()`, the extra `metadata` parameter creates a signature mismatch: `add_done_callback`
expects `Callable[[Task[T]], None]` but `process_result` has type `Callable[[Task[int], str], None]`. The AST oracle (Phase 0) identifies this as a PEP 484 argument type
violation. Mypy and zuban correctly flag the incompatible callback type, while pyrefly and ty accept it silently — another 2-2 split where the phased evaluation correctly
determines which checkers are right through structural analysis alone.

### TypeGuard with NewType in Generic Containers

The file `typeguard-generic-list-newtype`, seeded from mypy issue #18470, defines `TypeGuard` functions that narrow `List[U]` to `List[UserID]` or `List[ProductID]` where
`UserID` and `ProductID` are `NewType` wrappers. The interaction between TypeGuard's narrowing semantics and NewType's nominal typing creates a complex type flow: after the
guard, the list's element type should be narrowed to the specific NewType, but the generic `U` parameter complicates inference. Mypy correctly handles the narrowing and reports
errors where the narrowed type is used incorrectly. Pyrefly, zuban, and ty all miss the violation — making this a case where only the most mature checker gets it right.

### Match Exhaustiveness and Protocol Variance (PEPs 484, 544)

Two files test the intersection of structural match patterns with generic types and Protocols. In `match-typeguard-generic-tuple`, a function uses `TypeGuard` functions within
match case guards to narrow tuple types. The function declares a `str` return type but does not return a value on all code paths when the match statement is incomplete:

**Code Listing 4.4: Non-exhaustive match with TypeGuard narrowing.**
```{python}
def process_item[X, Y](
    item: Union[Tuple[int, bool], Tuple[str, str], Tuple[X, Y]]
) -> str:
    match item:
        case (a, b) if is_str_pair(item):
            return f"String pair: {a}, {b}"
        case (x, y) if is_int_bool_pair(item):
            return f"Int-bool pair: {x}, {y}"
        case (p, q):
            return f"Generic pair: {p}, {q}"
    # Missing return when no case matches
```

Mypy, pyrefly, and ty correctly report the missing return statement, but zuban does not — producing a false negative for a straightforward PEP 484 requirement that all code
paths must return a value when a return type is declared.

In `match-protocol-generic-type`, a Protocol `Operation[P_R]` with an invariant type variable is used as a function parameter `op: Operation[T]`, and match arms return
concrete types (`int`, `str`) where the generic `T` is declared. Mypy and zuban correctly report both the variance violation (PEP 544 requires Protocols to use appropriate
variance annotations) and the return type incompatibility (returning `int` where `T` is expected violates PEP 484). Pyrefly and ty miss both errors, demonstrating incomplete
support for the intersection of Protocol variance with pattern matching.

## Genuinely Uncertain Cases

Two files in our curated set receive UNCERTAIN verdicts for all four checkers, representing areas where the Python typing specification does not prescribe a single correct
behavior. These cases illustrate a fundamental limitation of automated type checker evaluation that connects to Rice's theorem [@rice1953classes]: any non-trivial semantic
property of a program is undecidable in general. Type checkers are heuristic approximations that make different trade-offs between soundness and completeness, and when the
specification is ambiguous about which trade-off is correct, no automated oracle — whether runtime-based, AST-based, or LLM-based — can definitively resolve the disagreement.
The challenge is compounded by the fact that type checker diagnostics are static warnings derived from heuristic inference, not provable facts. When two checkers infer different
types for the same expression and the specification does not prescribe a unique answer, the disagreement reflects a legitimate design difference rather than a bug in either
implementation.

### Unsound Cast with Self (PEP 673)

The file `unsound-self-cast-divergence` uses `cast(Self, ...)` to convert between `Node[int]` and `SpecialNode[int]` in a loop. The relevant pattern is:

**Code Listing 4.5: Unsound cast from base to subclass via Self.**
```{python}
class Node(Generic[T]):
    def get_next_or_default(self, default_val: T) -> Self:
        if self._next is not None:
            return cast(Self, self._next)  # Unsound: _next may be Node, not Self
        else:
            return type(self)(default_val)
```

This cast is inherently unsound — `self._next` is typed as `Node[T]` but `Self` may refer to `SpecialNode[T]`, a subclass with additional attributes. At runtime, accessing
`current_node.metadata` after the unsound cast will raise `AttributeError` if the actual object is a plain `Node`. However, PEP 484 explicitly states that `cast()` is not
checked at runtime and type checkers should trust it: "No runtime checking is performed... the type checker should suppress any errors on the expression." This creates a
genuine design tension: mypy and zuban follow the specification literally and trust the cast, while pyrefly and ty attempt to validate the cast's soundness. Neither approach is
definitively wrong — the specification intentionally leaves `cast()` unchecked, but a checker that warns about provably unsound casts provides additional safety. This
disagreement cannot be resolved by any tier of our evaluation system because it is a philosophical difference about the role of `cast()` in the type system, not a factual
question about what the code does.

### TypedDict total= Inheritance (PEP 589)

The file `typeddict-total-inheritance-divergence` defines a TypedDict hierarchy where `OptionalFeatures(TypedDict, total=False)` declares `debug_mode: bool` and
`log_level: Literal[...]`, and `FullServiceConfig(BaseConfig, OptionalFeatures, total=True)` overrides totality:

**Code Listing 4.6: TypedDict inheritance with conflicting totality.**
```{python}
class OptionalFeatures(TypedDict, total=False):
    debug_mode: bool
    log_level: Literal["INFO", "WARN", "ERROR"]

class FullServiceConfig(BaseConfig, OptionalFeatures, total=True):
    api_key: str
    timeout_seconds: NotRequired[int]
    # Are debug_mode and log_level Required or NotRequired here?
```

PEP 589 specifies that `total=True` makes all *newly declared* fields required, but the interaction between inherited fields from a `total=False` parent and the child's
`total=True` setting is underspecified. Mypy treats the inherited fields as Required (consistent with the child's `total=True`), reasoning that totality applies to the final
class's view of all its fields. Pyrefly and ty preserve the `NotRequired` status from the parent, reasoning that the original declaration's totality should be respected. Zuban
takes a third approach, treating some inherited fields differently depending on whether they have explicit `NotRequired` annotations. All four checkers produce subtly different
revealed types for `.get()` calls on the inherited fields, and no single behavior can be definitively identified as correct based on the current PEP text. This case exemplifies
how specification ambiguity — not implementation bugs — can be the root cause of type checker disagreements, and why a fully automated evaluation system must acknowledge its
own limits rather than force a verdict where none is warranted.

## Discussion

The results reveal a clear maturity gradient across the four evaluated type checkers. Mypy's 78.3% accuracy reflects over a decade of development and extensive conformance
testing, though it is not infallible — the Literal narrowing case demonstrates that even the most mature checker can miss valid type narrowing opportunities. Zuban's 69.6%
accuracy positions it as a competent alternative that handles most common patterns correctly but struggles with certain match exhaustiveness checks. Pyrefly's 52.2% accuracy
indicates significant gaps in TypeIs handling, TypedDict Unpack support, and Protocol variance checking, despite correct behavior on most Phase 1 crash cases. Ty's 4.5%
accuracy with only one CORRECT verdict across 22 decidable cases confirms that the alpha release lacks support for many advanced typing constructs that the other three
checkers handle correctly.

The phased evaluation system itself demonstrates a clear value hierarchy. Phase 1 (runtime crashes) resolved 9 of 25 files with the highest confidence. These verdicts are
undeniable because code either crashes or it does not. Phase 2 (Hypothesis) resolved an additional 4 files by discovering type violations through adversarial input generation
that the LLM-generated test harness may not have covered. Phase 0/3 (oracle and PEP compliance) resolved 10 files through static analysis and PEP matching. Only 2 of 25
files (8.0%) remained genuinely UNCERTAIN, both involving specification ambiguities rather than evaluation limitations. The system's ability to resolve six of eight 2-2
splits — cases where consensus-based approaches would be powerless — validates the design decision to use independent oracles rather than majority voting.

The TypedDict ecosystem emerges as a particularly rich area for disagreements. Seven of the 25 mutated files exercise TypedDict features, and they span the full confidence
spectrum from Phase 1 runtime crashes through Phase 3 PEP compliance to genuinely UNCERTAIN specification ambiguities. The three TypedDict Unpack files demonstrate a
gradient of checker competence: mypy catches all three violations, pyrefly and zuban catch one of three, and ty catches none. The TypedDict inheritance files expose a deeper
issue: the `total=` flag's interaction with inheritance is underspecified in PEP 589, leading to four different interpretations across the four checkers. This pattern
suggests that TypedDict support beyond basic field access, particularly the interaction with `Unpack`, `NotRequired`, `total`, and `extra_items`, remains an area of active
development across the ecosystem.

The three files where all four checkers fail (`inherited-unsound-helper`, `isinstance-protocol-divergence`, and `decorator-super-generic-args`) are arguably the most valuable
findings, as they expose blind spots shared across the entire ecosystem rather than individual checker weaknesses. These systemic gaps, unsound `Self` return types, Protocol
runtime-checkability, and `ParamSpec` argument forwarding, represent areas where the typing specification may benefit from additional clarity or where checkers should invest
in deeper semantic analysis.

## Threats to Validity

Several factors limit the generalizability of the findings this paper discovered. First, the curated set of 25 files is small relative to the full space of Python typing
constructs while the selected files cover the four major disagreement patterns identified in the research questions (TypedDict handling, generic Self types, NewType validation, and TypeGuard
soundness), other construct categories remain unexplored. Second, all four type checkers are under active development, and the version specific results reported here
(particularly for the alpha-stage ty) may not reflect the current state of these tools. We observed during our evaluation period that ty's conformance score improved across
successive releases, suggesting rapid progress.

Third, the exclusion of two Phase 2 files on the basis of questionable Hypothesis evidence introduces a selection effect. Our accuracy figures may overstate checker
performance if the excluded files would have produced additional INCORRECT verdicts. However, including files with unreliable evidence would undermine the credibility of the
curated set, and the author of this paper prioritizes verdict accuracy over sample size. Fourth, the LLM-generated test cases, while seeded from real bug reports, may
exercise typing constructs in patterns that differ from real world codebases. The presence of seed issue references for 15 of 25 files provides some assurance of practical
relevance, but the mutation process inevitably introduces artificial constructions that developers would rarely write.
